The paper provides formal definitions and examples of both the algebraic and logical descriptions, and discusses their relationship and applications in the study of generalized trees.

This paper presents algebraic and logical descriptions of generalized trees, which are a generalization of the classical tree structure. These descriptions provide a unified framework for reasoning about generalized trees, including their properties and operations. The algebraic description introduces the notion of a tree algebra, which captures the structure of a generalized tree as a mathematical object. This allows for the use of algebraic techniques to analyze and manipulate generalized trees. The logical description introduces a logical language for expressing properties of generalized trees, as well as a logical calculus to reason about these properties. Together, these descriptions provide a comprehensive and formal foundation for the study and analysis of generalized trees. The paper also includes examples and applications of these descriptions, illustrating their usefulness in various contexts.

This paper presents an analysis of Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound for nonlinear parameter estimation after compressed sensing. Compressed sensing is a technique used to efficiently acquire and reconstruct sparse signals. In this study, we consider the problem of estimating parameters in a nonlinear model from compressed measurements. We derive expressions for the Fisher information matrix and the Cramér-Rao bound for this scenario. We also investigate the impact of the measurement matrix and the sparsity level on the estimation performance. Our results show that the Fisher information and the Cramér-Rao bound depend on the measurement matrix and the sparsity level, and that the estimation performance can be improved by carefully designing the measurement matrix. These findings provide valuable insights for the design and analysis of compressed sensing systems for nonlinear parameter estimation.

This paper presents an analysis of the Fisher Information and the Cramér-Rao bound for nonlinear parameter estimation in the context of compressed sensing. Compressed sensing is a technique used to efficiently acquire and reconstruct sparse signals using a reduced number of measurements. The Fisher Information is a measure of the amount of information that an estimation procedure carries about the parameters being estimated. The Cramér-Rao bound is a fundamental limit on the accuracy of unbiased estimators. The authors derive expressions for the Fisher Information and the Cramér-Rao bound for nonlinear parameter estimation in compressed sensing scenarios. They also propose an algorithm for estimating the Fisher Information and demonstrate its effectiveness through simulations. The results show that compressed sensing can provide accurate parameter estimation even with a small number of measurements, as long as the Fisher Information is properly exploited. This work contributes to a deeper understanding of the theoretical limits and practical implications of parameter estimation in compressed sensing applications.

The paper presents a proof that the $\pi$-calculus, a process calculus used for modeling concurrent and distributed systems, is both behaviourally complete and orbit-finitely executable. Behavioural completeness means that the $\pi$-calculus can express any computable behaviour, while orbit-finite executability means that any $\pi$-calculus process can be executed in a finite number of steps. The proof is based on a novel encoding of the $\pi$-calculus into a variant of the $\lambda$-calculus, which allows for the translation of $\pi$-calculus processes into executable $\lambda$-calculus terms. The paper also discusses the implications of these results for the practical use of the $\pi$-calculus in the design and analysis of concurrent and distributed systems.

This paper proves that the $\pi$-calculus is behaviourally complete and orbit-finitely executable. The $\pi$-calculus, a process calculus that describes concurrent and distributed systems, has been widely studied in the field of computer science. In this paper, the authors apply a new technique called orbit-finite executability to the $\pi$-calculus, which allows them to analyse and reason about the behaviour of processes in an efficient and effective manner. The authors provide a formal definition of orbit-finite executability and prove that the $\pi$-calculus satisfies this property. They also demonstrate the usefulness of orbit-finite executability by presenting several examples and case studies. The results of this paper contribute to the understanding and application of the $\pi$-calculus in the field of computer science and provide new insights into the behaviour of concurrent and distributed systems.

This paper investigates the relationship between Petersen cores and the oddness of cubic graphs. A Petersen core is a subgraph of a cubic graph that is isomorphic to the Petersen graph. The oddness of a cubic graph is a measure of how many odd cycles it contains. The main result of this paper is that every cubic graph with an odd number of odd cycles contains a Petersen core. This result has implications for the study of cubic graphs and their properties. The paper also provides a proof of this result and discusses its significance in the context of other related results in graph theory.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Petersen cores and the oddness of cubic graphs" discusses the relationship between Petersen cores and the oddness of cubic graphs. The paper explores the concept of Petersen cores, which are certain subgraphs of cubic graphs that have specific properties. The authors introduce a new method for classifying these Petersen cores based on their oddness, or the number of odd cycles they contain. They then proceed to analyze the oddness of various types of cubic graphs, and prove that certain families of cubic graphs always contain Petersen cores with a certain oddness. The results of this research provide insight into the structure and properties of cubic graphs.

This paper investigates the homotopy invariants of combings of 3-manifolds. A combing is a way to assign a preferred direction to each point on a manifold. The authors define a new homotopy invariant called the combing group, which captures the essential information about the combing. They prove that the combing group is isomorphic to the fundamental group of the manifold, and show that it is a complete invariant for certain classes of 3-manifolds. The paper also explores the relationship between combings and other well-known invariants, such as the Thurston norm and the Heegaard genus. Overall, this work provides a deeper understanding of the structure and properties of combings of 3-manifolds.

This paper explores the concept of combings of 3-manifolds and the associated homotopy invariants. A combing of a 3-manifold is a way to assign a unique direction to each curve on the manifold. The authors define three different types of combings and investigate their properties. They then introduce various homotopy invariants that can be derived from these combings, including the Euler characteristic, the fundamental group, and the Alexander polynomial. The paper concludes with an analysis of the relationships between these invariants and the combings, providing insights into the topological structure of 3-manifolds.

This paper investigates the properties of edge-intersecting and non-splitting one bend paths in a grid. The authors define a graph representation for these paths and analyze their characteristics. They prove that the graph of edge-intersecting one bend paths is planar and has a linear number of vertices and edges. They also show that the graph of non-splitting one bend paths is a subgraph of the graph of edge-intersecting one bend paths. The authors provide algorithms for constructing these graphs and discuss their applications in various fields such as computer graphics and VLSI design. Overall, this paper contributes to the understanding of one bend paths in a grid and provides valuable insights for further research in this area.

This paper introduces the concept of edge-intersecting and non-splitting one bend paths in a grid. It presents a method to construct the corresponding graphs for these paths, which have applications in network design and routing. The paper also analyzes the properties and structures of these graphs, providing insights into their connectivity, diameter, and ultra-metrics. Additionally, the paper proposes an algorithm to efficiently determine the shortest edge-intersecting and non-splitting one bend paths in a grid. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithm. Overall, this paper contributes to the understanding and utilization of graphs of edge-intersecting and non-splitting one bend paths in grid-based systems.

In this paper, we investigate the valley polarization and valley coherence properties of bilayer WS2, a two-dimensional material with potential applications in valleytronics. We find that bilayer WS2 exhibits anomalously robust valley polarization, meaning that the polarization is maintained even at high temperatures and in the presence of disorder. We also observe long valley coherence times, indicating that the valley information can be preserved over long distances. These findings suggest that bilayer WS2 could be a promising material for valleytronic devices.

In this paper, we report on the experimental observation of anomalously robust valley polarization and valley coherence in bilayer WS2, a two-dimensional transition metal dichalcogenide material. By applying circularly polarized light and measuring the integrated photoluminescence intensity from the A and B excitons, we observe a strikingly high valley polarization and sustained valley coherence at room temperature. We also investigate the effects of temperature and external magnetic field on the valley properties, and find that the valley polarization and coherence remain robust under these conditions. Our findings provide crucial insights into the fundamental physics of valleytronics in bilayer WS2 and have potential implications for future valley-based applications in optoelectronics and quantum information processing.

This paper explores the concept of snow leopard permutations and their even and odd threads. Snow leopard permutations are a type of permutation that exhibit certain properties related to their even and odd elements. The authors provide a detailed analysis of these properties and present several theorems and proofs to support their findings. Additionally, the paper discusses the applications of snow leopard permutations in various fields, such as cryptography and computer science. Overall, this study contributes to the understanding of snow leopard permutations and their potential implications in different domains.

The paper discusses the concept of snow leopard permutations and their even and odd threads. Snow leopard permutations are a type of permutation where each of the elements is either positive or negative. The authors examine the properties and structure of snow leopard permutations and introduce the concept of even and odd threads within these permutations. Even threads are defined as substring sequences that contain an equal number of positive and negative elements, while odd threads contain an odd number of either positive or negative elements. The authors provide formal definitions and rules for even and odd threads within snow leopard permutations. Furthermore, they explore the algebraic properties of these permutations and discuss the implications and potential applications of their findings in various fields such as cryptography and combinatorics. Overall, the paper contributes to the understanding of snow leopard permutations and their underlying structure, highlighting the significance of even and odd threads in these permutations.

In this paper, we present a method for identifying the interval of parameters that govern the spin reorientation transition in (Ga,Mn)As. We use a combination of experimental measurements and theoretical calculations to determine the range of values for the ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) parameters that are consistent with the observed behavior of the system. Our approach involves measuring the FMR spectra at different temperatures and magnetic fields, and then fitting these data to a theoretical model. By systematically varying the parameters in the model, we are able to identify the range of values that produce the best fit to the experimental data. Our results provide valuable insights into the spin reorientation transition in (Ga,Mn)As and demonstrate the effectiveness of our method for parameter identification in complex magnetic systems.

In this paper, we present a new method for identifying the parameters that govern the spin reorientation transition in (Ga,Mn)As, a material with promising applications in spintronics. Our approach involves measuring the ferromagnetic resonance (FMR) response of the material at different temperatures and magnetic fields, and determining the intervals of parameter values that accurately reproduce the experimental data. By utilizing this new method, we are able to estimate the critical temperature and magnetic field at which the spin reorientation transition occurs in (Ga,Mn)As, providing valuable insights into the material's magnetic properties. This work contributes to a better understanding of (Ga,Mn)As and paves the way for future studies on spin reorientation transitions in similar materials.

In this paper, we establish a generalised comparison principle for the Monge-Ampère equation and the pressure in two-dimensional fluid flows. We first introduce the Monge-Ampère equation and its physical interpretation in fluid dynamics. We then prove the comparison principle for the Monge-Ampère equation, which states that if two solutions of the equation satisfy certain conditions, then the larger solution is greater than or equal to the smaller solution. We also extend this comparison principle to the pressure in two-dimensional fluid flows. Our results provide a useful tool for studying the behavior of solutions to the Monge-Ampère equation and the pressure in fluid flows, and have potential applications in various fields such as meteorology and oceanography.

This paper introduces a generalised comparison principle for the Monge-Amp\`ere equation and the pressure in 2D fluid flows. The Monge-Amp\`ere equation is a nonlinear partial differential equation that arises in various areas of mathematics and physics. The comparison principle is a fundamental tool for studying solutions to this equation, and the authors provide a generalised version that extends to the pressure in 2D fluid flows. The authors establish the existence and uniqueness of solutions to the generalised comparison principle and discuss its applications in the study of fluid dynamics. This generalisation allows for a more comprehensive understanding of the Monge-Amp\`ere equation and its relationship to fluid flows in a two-dimensional setting.

In this paper, we establish a correspondence between the dynamics of unicritical polynomials and the Galois theory of their iterates. We show that the dynamics of a unicritical polynomial can be understood in terms of the Galois group of its iterates, and vice versa. Specifically, we prove that the Galois group of the iterates of a unicritical polynomial determines the dynamics of the polynomial, and conversely, the dynamics of the polynomial determine the Galois group of its iterates. We provide explicit formulas and algorithms for computing the Galois group of the iterates of a given unicritical polynomial, and we demonstrate the usefulness of our correspondence in studying the dynamics of unicritical polynomials. Our results have implications for the study of complex dynamics and Galois theory, and they shed light on the interplay between these two areas of mathematics.

This paper presents a comprehensive study on the relationship between Galois groups and dynamical systems associated with unicritical polynomials. The main result establishes a correspondence between the classification of Galois groups of iterates of a polynomial and the dynamics of its associated dynamical system. Building upon previous work on the relationship between Galois theory and complex dynamics, the authors provide a detailed proof of this correspondence and explore its implications for the study of unicritical polynomials. The findings of this research contribute to the understanding of the interplay between algebra and dynamics in the context of unicritical polynomials.

This paper explores the implications of the discovery of the Higgs boson on the theory of positively deflected anomaly mediation. Anomaly mediation is a mechanism that explains the hierarchy problem in particle physics. The authors analyze how the Higgs boson discovery affects the predictions of positively deflected anomaly mediation and find that it leads to a more consistent and accurate description of the observed data. The paper concludes by discussing the potential implications of these findings for future research in particle physics.

In this academic paper, we explore the consequences of the discovery of the Higgs boson on anomaly mediation in particle physics. Specifically, we focus on the phenomenon of positively deflected anomaly mediation, which refers to the modification of the mediation of supersymmetry breaking by the presence of the Higgs boson. We discuss the implications of this discovery on the stability of supersymmetry and the nature of the Higgs boson itself. Our analysis reveals that positively deflected anomaly mediation can provide a natural explanation for the observed properties of the Higgs boson, while also addressing some of the theoretical challenges in supersymmetry. We conclude by highlighting the potential implications of this discovery for future research in particle physics and the development of new theoretical frameworks.

We investigate the remote transfer of Gaussian quantum discord between two distant parties connected by a noisy quantum channel. We consider a bipartite Gaussian state shared between Alice and Bob, and we aim to transfer the quantum discord from Alice's side to Bob's side. We find that the transfer of quantum discord is possible even in the presence of noise, and we provide a protocol for achieving this transfer. We analyze the performance of the protocol in terms of the fidelity of the transferred quantum discord and the amount of noise in the channel. Our results show that the transfer of quantum discord can be achieved with high fidelity even in the presence of moderate noise levels. This work contributes to the understanding of the remote transfer of quantum correlations and has potential applications in quantum communication and quantum information processing.

In this paper, we propose a protocol for the remote transfer of Gaussian quantum discord, a measure of quantum correlations, between two distant parties connected by a quantum channel. We show that it is possible to remotely generate and transfer Gaussian quantum discord by applying local operations and classical communication at both ends of the channel. We derive the conditions under which the transfer can be achieved and analyze the role of different parameters such as the channel transmissivity and the quantum noise. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of remote transfer of quantum discord and provide insights into the dynamics of quantum correlations in the presence of noise. This work has potential applications in quantum communication and information processing tasks that rely on quantum correlations.

Quantum random number generators (QRNGs) have gained significant attention due to their inherent randomness and potential applications in cryptography and simulation. However, existing QRNG designs often suffer from limitations such as low speed, high cost, and vulnerability to external disturbances. In this paper, we propose a minimalist design for a robust real-time QRNG that addresses these limitations. Our design utilizes a single-photon source, a beam splitter, and a single-photon detector, resulting in a simple and cost-effective setup. We demonstrate the robustness of our design by conducting extensive experiments under various environmental conditions and external disturbances. The results show that our QRNG achieves high-speed generation of random numbers with excellent statistical properties, while remaining resilient to external disturbances. Our minimalist design offers a promising solution for practical implementation of QRNGs in various applications.

This paper proposes a minimalist design of a robust real-time quantum random number generator (RQRNG). The design is based on the principles of quantum mechanics and utilizes the inherent randomness of quantum phenomena. The proposed RQRNG consists of a single photon source, a beam splitter, and two single photon detectors. We demonstrate that the design provides true randomness and has excellent statistical properties. The implemented RQRNG achieves a high bit rate and low bias, making it suitable for a wide range of applications. The simplicity and robustness of the design make it a practical solution for generating random numbers in real-time.

This paper presents fast solvers for unsteady thermal fluid structure interaction (FSU-TFSI) problems. The FSU-TFSI problems involve the coupling of fluid dynamics, heat transfer, and structural mechanics, and are commonly encountered in various engineering applications. The proposed fast solvers aim to reduce the computational cost associated with solving these problems by exploiting the inherent structure of the governing equations. Specifically, the authors develop a parallel-in-time algorithm based on the Parareal method, which allows for the simultaneous solution of multiple time steps. Additionally, a domain decomposition method is employed to distribute the computational workload across multiple processors. The effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed solvers are demonstrated through numerical experiments on a range of benchmark problems. The results show that the FSU-TFSI problems can be solved with significantly reduced computational time, making them more accessible for practical engineering applications.

This paper presents a novel approach to solving unsteady thermal fluid structure interaction problems using fast solvers. The traditional approach to solving these problems involves the use of explicit time integration schemes, which are computationally expensive and time-consuming. In this paper, we propose a new method that combines the advantages of implicit and explicit solvers, resulting in significant computational speed-up. The proposed method is based on a modified version of the projection method and uses a preconditioned conjugate gradient solver to efficiently solve the resulting linear systems. We demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach through numerical experiments on a range of test problems. Our results show that the proposed method achieves accurate and fast solutions, making it a promising tool for solving unsteady thermal fluid structure interaction problems.

This paper investigates the impact of electrode density of states (DOS) on the transport properties of pyridine-linked single molecule junctions. The authors use a combination of density functional theory (DFT) calculations and non-equilibrium Green's function (NEGF) formalism to study the electronic structure and transport characteristics of these junctions. They find that the DOS of the electrodes strongly influences the conductance and transmission spectra of the junctions. Specifically, they observe that the presence of unoccupied states in the electrode DOS leads to resonant transport through the pyridine molecule. The authors also explore the effect of varying the electrode DOS on the conductance and transmission spectra, and discuss the implications of their findings for the design and optimization of molecular electronic devices. Overall, this study provides valuable insights into the role of electrode DOS in determining the transport properties of pyridine-linked single molecule junctions.

In this paper, the authors investigate the impact of the density of states (DOS) of the electrodes on the transport properties of pyridine-linked single molecule junctions. By varying the energy levels of the electrode's DOS and the alignment between the molecule's energy levels and the electrode's DOS, the authors demonstrate how the transport characteristics of the junctions can be manipulated. The experiments, performed using a combination of scanning tunneling microscopy and spectroscopy, reveal that the DOS of the electrodes directly influences the conductance and rectification behavior of the junctions. The findings provide valuable insights into the fundamental mechanisms governing charge transport through single molecule junctions and have implications for the design and optimization of molecular electronic devices.

This paper explores the concept of quantitative automata under probabilistic semantics. It presents a formal framework for modeling and analyzing systems that exhibit both probabilistic and quantitative behavior. The paper introduces a new class of automata called quantitative automata, which extend traditional automata models to incorporate quantitative aspects such as costs, rewards, and probabilities. The authors define the semantics of quantitative automata using probabilistic transition systems and provide algorithms for analyzing their behavior. The paper also discusses applications of quantitative automata in various domains, including performance analysis, optimization, and decision-making. Overall, this paper contributes to the understanding and development of quantitative automata as a powerful tool for modeling and analyzing complex systems.

The abstract of the academic paper titled "Quantitative Automata under Probabilistic Semantics" provides an overview of the paper's main subject and findings. The paper focuses on the study of quantitative automata, a formal model used in areas such as verification and synthesis of systems with quantitative aspects. It introduces a new probabilistic semantics for quantitative automata, which allows for the specification of quantitative properties in a probabilistic setting. The paper investigates the expressiveness and decidability of this new model, proving that it is more expressive than existing models and that the satisfiability problem for quantitative automata under probabilistic semantics is decidable. Furthermore, the paper presents algorithms for model-checking and synthesizing quantitative automata with probabilistic semantics. Experimental results demonstrate the usability and efficiency of these algorithms. Overall, this paper contributes to the field of formal methods and provides a foundation for future research in quantitative automata under probabilistic semantics.

The crystallographic phase problem is a fundamental challenge in electron diffraction, where the phase information of the diffracted waves is lost during the measurement process. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to solve the phase problem using dynamical scattering in electron diffraction. We demonstrate the feasibility of our method through simulations and experimental results. Our approach offers a promising solution to the long-standing phase problem in electron diffraction, opening up new possibilities for high-resolution structural analysis of materials.

The crystallographic phase problem is a longstanding challenge in structural biology and materials science. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to solving this problem using dynamical scattering in electron diffraction. By exploiting the wave nature of electrons and their interaction with crystals, we demonstrate that it is feasible to extract phase information from electron diffraction patterns. We present a detailed theoretical framework for this approach and validate it through simulations and experimental results. Our findings open up new possibilities for solving the phase problem in electron diffraction, which could have significant implications for the field of crystallography.

This paper presents a novel approach to operational concurrency control in distributed systems that can handle arbitrary scale and latency. Traditional concurrency control mechanisms often struggle to maintain consistency and performance in large-scale distributed systems with high latency. The proposed approach leverages a combination of optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control techniques to achieve both strong consistency and high performance. The system dynamically adapts its concurrency control strategy based on the current scale and latency of the system. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms existing concurrency control mechanisms in terms of both throughput and latency, making it suitable for large-scale distributed systems with varying levels of latency.

This academic paper focuses on the challenges of enforcing concurrency control in large-scale distributed systems with varying latency. The authors propose a new approach, called Operational Concurrency Control (OCC), that aims to minimize conflicts and ensure consistency while maintaining high performance. They present a detailed analysis of the OCC algorithm and its implementation, evaluating its effectiveness through experiments on a simulated distributed system. The results demonstrate the scalability and efficiency of OCC compared to traditional concurrency control mechanisms. Overall, this paper offers insights into how concurrency control can be effectively managed in distributed systems with arbitrary scale and latency.

This paper presents a demonstration of spectral level reconstruction of intrinsic $B$-mode power. The $B$-mode power spectrum is an important quantity in cosmology as it contains information about the early universe and the nature of dark matter. However, the measurement of the $B$-mode power spectrum is challenging due to contamination from various sources such as instrumental noise and foregrounds. In this paper, we propose a method to reconstruct the intrinsic $B$-mode power spectrum by removing the contamination using a spectral level reconstruction technique. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method using simulated data and show that it can accurately recover the underlying $B$-mode power spectrum. Our results suggest that spectral level reconstruction can be a valuable tool for improving the measurement of the $B$-mode power spectrum and advancing our understanding of the early universe.

In this paper, we present a demonstration of spectral level reconstruction of intrinsic $B$-mode power. We propose a novel method that allows for the reconstruction of the intrinsic $B$-mode power spectrum from temperature and polarization data. Our method involves decomposing the power spectrum into a sum of spectral components and calibrating these components using known theoretical models. We show that our method can accurately reconstruct the intrinsic $B$-mode power spectrum, thereby providing valuable insights into the underlying physics of the cosmic microwave background radiation. Overall, our demonstration highlights the potential of spectral level reconstruction as a powerful tool for analyzing and interpreting large-scale cosmological data sets.

This paper presents a new universal parallel two-pass MDL context tree compression algorithm. The algorithm is designed to improve the compression performance of context tree models by utilizing parallel processing techniques. The algorithm is based on the minimum description length (MDL) principle, which aims to find the most compact representation of the data. The proposed algorithm consists of two passes: a forward pass and a backward pass. In the forward pass, the algorithm constructs a context tree model based on the input data. In the backward pass, the algorithm refines the context tree model by iteratively removing unnecessary nodes. The experimental results show that the proposed algorithm achieves better compression performance compared to existing algorithms. Additionally, the algorithm is highly parallelizable, allowing for efficient compression on multi-core processors. Overall, the proposed algorithm provides a promising approach for improving the compression performance of context tree models.

This paper presents a novel parallel two-pass context tree compression algorithm utilizing the Minimum Description Length (MDL) principle. The proposed algorithm is capable of achieving high compression ratios while maintaining a low computational complexity. The algorithm utilizes a parallel processing approach to improve the compression speed without compromising its performance. Additionally, the algorithm is shown to be universally applicable to various types of data, making it highly versatile. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, outperforming existing context tree-based compression algorithms in terms of both compression ratio and speed. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive exploration of a parallel two-pass MDL context tree compression algorithm, highlighting its potential for practical data compression applications.

This paper presents efficient approximation algorithms for multicell coordinated beamforming with a rate outage constraint. The authors propose two algorithms, namely the greedy algorithm and the subgradient algorithm, which aim to maximize the sum rate of all users in a multicell network while ensuring that the rate outage constraint is satisfied. The greedy algorithm iteratively selects the best beamforming vectors for each user, while the subgradient algorithm updates the beamforming vectors based on the subgradient of the objective function. The authors provide theoretical analysis and simulation results to demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the proposed algorithms. The results show that both algorithms achieve near-optimal performance with significantly reduced computational complexity compared to existing algorithms.

Abstract: This paper presents efficient approximation algorithms for multicell coordinated beamforming with a rate outage constraint. The objective of the problem is to maximize the sum-rate of a multicell network, while satisfying a constraint on the minimum rate requirement for each link. The proposed algorithms provide near-optimal solutions with low computational complexity. The key idea is to decompose the problem into smaller subproblems and solve them iteratively. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed algorithms achieve close-to-optimal performance with significantly reduced computational overhead compared to existing approaches. The proposed algorithms are applicable to practical multicell networks and can improve the system throughput by efficiently allocating wireless resources.

This paper presents a study on the use of sparse dictionaries for online learning with kernels. The authors propose a novel algorithm that combines the benefits of sparse coding and kernel methods to improve the efficiency and accuracy of online learning tasks. The algorithm is evaluated on various datasets and compared with existing methods. The results show that the proposed algorithm outperforms other approaches in terms of both prediction accuracy and computational efficiency. The findings of this study contribute to the field of online learning and provide insights into the use of sparse dictionaries for kernel-based methods.

In this paper, we analyze the performance of sparse dictionaries for online learning with kernels. We consider the problem of learning a linear predictor in an online setting, where the input feature space is transformed using a kernel function. Our goal is to understand the impact of different dictionary designs on the performance of the online learning algorithm. Specifically, we consider two types of dictionaries: random Fourier features (RFF) and explicit feature maps. We analyze the theoretical guarantees for the prediction accuracy of these dictionaries, as well as their computational efficiency. Furthermore, we conduct experiments on various datasets to evaluate the empirical performance of these dictionaries. Our results show that RFF dictionaries can achieve comparable performance to explicit feature maps with significantly lower computational cost, making them a practical and efficient choice for online learning with kernels.

This paper explores the concept of similar submodules and coincidence site modules (CSMs) in crystallography. Similar submodules are defined as submodules that have the same orientation relationship with the parent lattice, while CSMs are defined as regions in the lattice where two different submodules coincide. The authors present a mathematical framework for characterizing and analyzing similar submodules and CSMs, and discuss their relevance in various crystallographic applications. The paper also provides experimental results and case studies to illustrate the practical implications of similar submodules and CSMs in materials science and engineering. Overall, this paper contributes to a deeper understanding of the structural properties of crystals and provides insights into their behavior under different conditions.

This academic paper explores the concept of similar submodules and coincidence site modules (CSMs). It highlights the importance of studying these modules in the field of crystallography and materials science. The paper explains the definitions and characteristics of similar submodules and CSMs, and provides examples of their applications in various crystal lattice structures. The paper also discusses the mathematical algorithms and techniques used to identify and analyze these modules. Furthermore, the paper discusses the role of similar submodules and CSMs in understanding crystal defects, interfaces, and phase boundaries. The findings of this research enhance our understanding of crystallography and materials science, and can potentially lead to advancements in materials engineering and design.

Residual amplitude modulation (RAM) is a significant noise source in interferometric gravitational wave detectors, which can limit their sensitivity. In this paper, we investigate the origins and characteristics of RAM in these detectors. We analyze the effects of various factors, such as laser frequency noise, optical path length fluctuations, and scattering, on the generation of RAM. We also propose and evaluate different mitigation techniques to reduce RAM and improve the sensitivity of the detectors. Our findings provide valuable insights into the understanding and control of RAM in interferometric gravitational wave detectors, which can contribute to the advancement of gravitational wave astronomy.

Residual amplitude modulation (RAM) is a common phenomenon in interferometric gravitational wave (GW) detectors that can significantly degrade the sensitivity of these instruments. RAM arises from various sources, including imperfect optics, non-linearities in the photo-detectors, and environmental disturbances. In this paper, we present a comprehensive review and analysis of RAM in GW detectors, focusing on its origin, measurement techniques, and mitigation strategies. We discuss the impact of RAM on the sensitivity of GW detectors and propose several approaches to mitigate this effect, including active feedback control systems and improved optical designs. Through numerical simulations and experimental data, we demonstrate the effectiveness of these mitigation strategies and provide recommendations for future developments in the field. This paper serves as a valuable resource for researchers and engineers involved in the design and operation of interferometric GW detectors, helping to improve their sensitivity and detection capabilities.

In this paper, we present a large-scale pattern observed in the optical polarization vectors of quasars. Quasars are highly luminous and distant celestial objects that emit large amounts of energy. The polarization of light from quasars can provide valuable information about the physical processes occurring in these objects. We analyzed a large dataset of quasar polarization measurements and found a systematic alignment of polarization vectors on scales of several degrees. This alignment is not expected from random fluctuations and suggests the presence of a coherent physical mechanism. We discuss possible explanations for this pattern, including the influence of magnetic fields or the alignment of quasar accretion disks. Our findings have important implications for our understanding of quasar physics and the role of polarization in studying these objects.

This paper presents a study on the large-scale pattern of optical quasar polarization vectors. The authors analyze a dataset of quasars observed over a period of five years, obtaining polarization measurements for a large sample of quasars. They identify a significant and coherent pattern in the polarization vectors, suggesting the presence of a large-scale magnetic field structure in the region surrounding the quasars. The authors discuss possible explanations for this pattern, including the influence of the intergalactic medium and the presence of magnetic fields in galaxy clusters. Overall, this study provides new insights into the nature of quasar polarization and sheds light on the physical processes occurring in the vicinity of these astrophysical objects.

This paper presents a framework for certified self-stabilization, which is a property of distributed systems that allows them to recover from any transient fault and reach a correct state in a finite amount of time. The framework consists of a set of rules and guidelines that can be used to design and analyze self-stabilizing algorithms. The authors provide a formal definition of self-stabilization and discuss the challenges and benefits of using this approach. They also present several case studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in designing self-stabilizing algorithms for different types of distributed systems. The paper concludes with a discussion on future research directions and potential applications of certified self-stabilization.

This paper presents a framework for certified self-stabilization, which is a property that allows systems to automatically recover to a correct state regardless of their initial state. The framework consists of a method for constructing self-stabilizing algorithms and a process for certifying their correctness. The method involves defining a set of local predicates that define correct states, and using transition rules to ensure that the system always progresses towards these states. The certification process involves proving that the self-stabilizing algorithm satisfies a given correctness specification, such as safety or liveness. The framework is demonstrated on several case studies, including distributed mutual exclusion and leader election algorithms, to show its effectiveness in constructing and certifying self-stabilizing systems.

This paper investigates the concept of an irreversible 2-conversion set in graphs of bounded degree. A 2-conversion set is a subset of vertices in a graph such that any two vertices in the set can be converted into each other by a sequence of at most two edge modifications. The authors prove that for graphs of bounded degree, there exists an irreversible 2-conversion set if and only if the graph contains a certain subgraph. They also provide an algorithm for finding such a subgraph in polynomial time. The results of this paper have implications for understanding the structure and dynamics of graphs with bounded degree.

This paper examines the concept of an irreversible 2-conversion set in graphs of bounded degree. An irreversible 2-conversion set is a subset of vertices in a graph such that no vertex in the set can be converted to any other vertex outside the set using a maximum of two conversion steps. The paper explores the properties of irreversible 2-conversion sets in graphs of bounded degree and provides conditions for their existence. Additionally, the paper discusses algorithms and computational complexity for finding irreversible 2-conversion sets in such graphs. The results presented in this paper contribute to the understanding of the structural properties of graphs and have potential applications in network optimization and algorithm design.

This paper presents a novel approach for soft-thresholding in both discrete and continuous-time domains with dynamic inputs. The proposed method utilizes a dynamic thresholding function that adapts to the changing input signal. The discrete-time soft-thresholding algorithm is derived using a recursive formulation, while the continuous-time soft-thresholding algorithm is obtained by solving a differential equation. The performance of the proposed algorithms is evaluated through simulations and compared with existing methods. The results demonstrate that the dynamic soft-thresholding approach outperforms traditional methods in terms of signal recovery and noise reduction. This research contributes to the field of signal processing by providing a new framework for soft-thresholding that can effectively handle dynamic inputs.

In many signal processing and machine learning applications, the problem of soft-thresholding is crucial for accurately recovering sparse signal representations. Existing methods for soft-thresholding often assume the availability of static input data, which limits their applicability to dynamic systems with changing inputs. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to soft-thresholding that can handle dynamic inputs in both discrete and continuous time. We present a mathematical framework for this dynamic soft-thresholding problem, and then develop efficient algorithms for both discrete and continuous-time settings. Our experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms existing approaches in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. Overall, our work provides a valuable contribution to the field of signal processing and machine learning by addressing the challenges of soft-thresholding in dynamic systems.

This academic paper investigates the factors that contribute to success in a single-elimination tournament. The study examines various characteristics of participants, such as age, gender, skill level, and experience, and analyzes their impact on tournament outcomes. The research utilizes a large dataset of tournament results from various sports and games to identify patterns and trends. The findings suggest that certain factors, such as skill level and experience, significantly increase the likelihood of winning a single-elimination tournament. The paper concludes with implications for tournament organizers and participants, highlighting the importance of considering these factors when designing and participating in such events.

This academic paper examines the factors that influence the outcome of single-elimination tournaments and predicts which types of teams or individuals are more likely to win such tournaments. The study analyzes historical data from various sports, including basketball, soccer, and tennis, to identify the characteristics that are associated with successful performance in single-elimination settings. The findings suggest that teams or individuals with higher skill levels, experience in high-pressure situations, and effective strategic planning are more likely to prevail in single-elimination tournaments. The paper also discusses the limitations of the study and suggests areas for further research to expand our understanding of the factors influencing tournament outcomes. Overall, this research provides valuable insights into the dynamics of single-elimination tournaments and offers practical implications for coaches, players, and tournament organizers.

Hyperspectral unmixing is a challenging task in remote sensing, where the goal is to estimate the abundance fractions of different materials present in a mixed pixel. Traditional unmixing methods often assume that the abundance fractions are spatially independent, which may not hold true in many real-world scenarios. In this paper, we propose a collaborative sparse regression approach that takes into account the spatial correlation of the abundance fractions. We model the spatial correlation using a graph-based representation and incorporate it into a sparse regression framework. Experimental results on real hyperspectral datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method in improving the unmixing accuracy compared to traditional methods that do not consider spatial correlation. Our approach has the potential to enhance the interpretation and analysis of hyperspectral imagery in various applications such as land cover classification and environmental monitoring.

Sparse regression is a widely used technique in hyperspectral unmixing, which aims to estimate the abundances of different materials present in a scene based on their spectral signatures. However, conventional sparse regression methods do not take into account the spatial correlation between the supports of the different materials, which can lead to inaccurate and unstable estimates. In this paper, we propose a novel collaborative sparse regression framework that incorporates spatial information into the estimation process. Specifically, we model the supports of the different materials as spatially correlated random variables and exploit this correlation to improve the estimation accuracy. We formulate the problem as a joint estimation and optimization problem, and propose an efficient algorithm to solve it. Experimental results on synthetic and real hyperspectral data demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach in improving the estimation accuracy compared to conventional sparse regression methods.

In this paper, we present a study on the application of van der Waals density-functional theory (vdW-DF) to bulk solids with body-centered cubic (BCC), face-centered cubic (FCC), and diamond structures. We investigate the accuracy of the vdW-DF method in predicting the lattice constants, cohesive energies, and bulk moduli of these materials. Our results show that the vdW-DF method provides accurate predictions for the lattice constants and cohesive energies of BCC and FCC structures, but underestimates the bulk moduli. For diamond structures, the vdW-DF method overestimates the lattice constant and cohesive energy, but provides a reasonable prediction for the bulk modulus. Overall, our study demonstrates the potential of vdW-DF method in accurately predicting the properties of bulk solids with different crystal structures.

In this paper, we present a study on bulk solids with body-centered cubic (BCC), face-centered cubic (FCC), and diamond structures using van der Waals density-functional theory (DFT). We calculate the lattice constants, cohesive energies, and bulk modulus for each structure, and compare the results with experimental data and other theoretical calculations. Our calculations demonstrate the accuracy and reliability of van der Waals DFT in predicting the properties of these bulk solids. The findings provide valuable insights into the application of van der Waals DFT in studying materials with different crystal structures.

This paper introduces the concept of weighted Sobolev spaces on metric measure spaces. The authors define these spaces using a weight function that takes into account the geometry and measure of the space. They establish the basic properties of these spaces, including their completeness and compactness. The authors also provide examples of metric measure spaces where these weighted Sobolev spaces are useful, such as in the study of partial differential equations. Overall, this paper contributes to the understanding and application of Sobolev spaces on metric measure spaces.

In this paper, we propose a method to define and study weighted Sobolev spaces on metric measure spaces. We establish the necessary tools and techniques to construct and analyze these spaces, including the definition of a weighted gradient and a weighted function space. We prove the existence and uniqueness of solutions to certain types of partial differential equations in these weighted Sobolev spaces. Using these results, we also establish various properties of these spaces, such as compactness and embedding theorems. Finally, we provide some examples and applications of these weighted Sobolev spaces in the field of geometric analysis. Overall, our work provides a solid foundation for the study of weighted Sobolev spaces on metric measure spaces and contributes to the understanding of these spaces in the context of partial differential equations.

This paper investigates the third unramified cohomology group of universal torsors over rational surfaces. The authors provide a detailed analysis of the structure and properties of this cohomology group, including its relation to other cohomology groups and its role in the classification of torsors. The paper also presents several examples and applications of this cohomology group in various areas of mathematics. Overall, this paper contributes to a deeper understanding of the cohomology theory of torsors over rational surfaces and its implications in different mathematical contexts.

This academic paper presents a study of the third unramified cohomology group of universal torsors over rational surfaces. The authors investigate the structure and properties of this cohomology group, focusing on the case of rational surfaces. They provide explicit calculations and examples, shedding light on the behavior of the third unramified cohomology group in this particular setting. The results of this study may have implications for the understanding of torsors and their applications in algebraic geometry.

This paper investigates the ion transport properties in macroscopic radio frequency (RF) linear traps. The authors present a theoretical model that describes the ion motion in these traps and analyze the effects of various parameters on the ion transport efficiency. They also experimentally validate the model by measuring the ion transport in a fabricated RF linear trap. The results show that the ion transport efficiency is influenced by the trap geometry, RF voltage amplitude, and frequency. The findings of this study provide valuable insights into the design and optimization of RF linear traps for ion manipulation and control.

This academic paper investigates the ion transport properties in macroscopic RF linear traps. The study focuses on understanding the transport dynamics and optimizing the trapping efficiency in these traps. Experimental measurements and simulations are performed to characterize the ion trajectories and analyze the effects of trap parameters on ion transport. The results show that ion transport in macroscopic RF linear traps is highly dependent on trap geometry, ion mass, and RF amplitude. Additionally, strategies for improving ion transport are discussed, including optimizing the trap design and applying electric fields to assist ion movement. The findings of this study contribute to the development and optimization of macroscopic RF linear traps, which are widely used in fields such as ion trapping, quantum computing, and mass spectrometry.

This paper provides an overview of flux braiding experiments, a technique used in plasma physics to study the behavior of magnetic fields in laboratory settings. The paper begins by explaining the concept of flux braiding and its relevance to understanding the dynamics of magnetic fields in astrophysical and fusion plasma systems. It then reviews various experimental setups and techniques used to create and manipulate braided magnetic fields, including magnetic reconnection, magnetic helicity injection, and magnetic relaxation. The paper also discusses the diagnostic tools and measurements used to study the resulting plasma dynamics and the insights gained from these experiments. Finally, the paper concludes with a discussion of the challenges and future directions in flux braiding experiments, including the need for improved diagnostic techniques and a better understanding of the underlying physics. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of flux braiding experiments and their contributions to the field of plasma physics.

This paper presents an overview of flux braiding experiments, a field of research that investigates the manipulation and braiding of magnetic fluids. The paper provides an introduction to the concept of flux braiding and the physical principles behind it. It reviews various experimental techniques and setups that have been used to study flux braiding, including magnetic field manipulations, fluid systems, and visualization methods. The paper also discusses the significance and potential applications of flux braiding experiments in fields such as magnetic reconnection, plasma physics, and fluid dynamics. Finally, it highlights current challenges and future directions for further research in the area of flux braiding.

This paper presents a method for identifying linear regressions with errors in all variables. The method is based on the assumption that the errors in the dependent variable and the independent variables are uncorrelated. The authors propose a two-step procedure for estimating the parameters of the regression model. In the first step, they estimate the parameters of a reduced form equation that includes the dependent variable and the errors in the independent variables. In the second step, they estimate the parameters of the structural equation that includes the dependent variable and the errors in the dependent variable. The authors provide theoretical results on the consistency and asymptotic normality of the proposed estimators. They also conduct a simulation study to assess the finite sample properties of the estimators. The results show that the proposed method performs well in terms of bias and efficiency. Overall, this paper provides a useful tool for identifying linear regressions with errors in all variables.

This paper proposes a new method for the identification of linear regressions in the presence of errors in all variables. Existing methods often assume errors in the dependent variable only, which limits their applicability. Our method extends these models to incorporate errors in both dependent and independent variables, thereby allowing for more accurate estimation and prediction. We derive necessary conditions for the identification of such models and provide practical guidelines for their implementation. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated through simulation studies and real data examples. The results show that considering errors in all variables improves the accuracy and efficiency of regression models. Overall, this paper contributes to the existing literature on regression analysis by addressing the issue of errors in all variables and providing a reliable method for their identification and estimation.

In this paper, we investigate the consistency of matter models with asymptotically safe quantum gravity. We consider a class of matter models that are coupled to gravity and study their behavior in the ultraviolet regime. We show that these matter models can be consistently coupled to asymptotically safe quantum gravity, which is a theory that remains well-defined at high energies. We analyze the renormalization group flow of the matter models and find that they exhibit a non-trivial fixed point in the ultraviolet regime. This fixed point allows for a consistent coupling between matter and gravity, providing a promising framework for studying the physics of the early universe and black holes. Our results suggest that matter models with asymptotically safe quantum gravity can provide a consistent description of fundamental interactions at high energies.

This paper examines the consistency of matter models with asymptotically safe quantum gravity. The authors argue that matter models that are required to be consistent with asymptotically safe quantum gravity should satisfy certain conditions. These conditions include the asymptotic safety condition, the effective average action, and the gravitational coupling. The authors analyze various matter models, such as scalar fields, gauge fields, and fermions, and determine whether they satisfy these conditions. They also discuss the implications of consistent matter models for the UV behavior of quantum gravity and the renormalization group flow. Overall, the paper concludes that certain matter models are consistent with asymptotically safe quantum gravity and provide important insights into the UV behavior of the theory. This has significant implications for the field of quantum gravity and the understanding of the fundamental nature of matter.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "Anisotropic magnetic properties of URhIn$_{5}$ compound" provides a summary of the research conducted on the magnetic properties of the URhIn$_{5}$ compound. The paper investigates the anisotropic behavior of the compound's magnetic properties, specifically focusing on the temperature and magnetic field dependence. The research findings reveal that the compound exhibits a complex magnetic behavior with multiple magnetic transitions and anisotropic behavior. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of these findings and their potential applications in the field of magnetism.

The abstract summarizes the content of the academic paper titled "Anisotropic magnetic properties of URhIn$_{5}$ compound". The paper investigates the magnetic properties of the URhIn$_{5}$ compound using various techniques such as magnetization measurements, specific heat measurements, and neutron diffraction experiments. The results show that the magnetic properties of URhIn$_{5}$ are highly anisotropic, with different magnetic orientations and behaviors along different crystallographic directions. The study also examines the effect of temperature and magnetic field on the anisotropic magnetic behavior of URhIn$_{5}$. The findings provide valuable insights into the magnetic properties of URhIn$_{5}$ and contribute to a better understanding of the behavior of this compound.

Superconductivity is a phenomenon in which certain materials exhibit zero electrical resistance at low temperatures. In this study, we investigate the superconductivity of niobium (Nb) thin films in BiOCl/Nb heterostructures. The heterostructures were fabricated using a pulsed laser deposition technique, and their structural and electrical properties were characterized using various techniques. We found that the superconducting transition temperature (Tc) of the Nb thin films in the heterostructures was significantly higher than that of bulk Nb. This enhancement in Tc can be attributed to the proximity effect, where the superconductivity of the Nb thin films is influenced by the BiOCl layer. Our results suggest that BiOCl/Nb heterostructures have potential applications in high-temperature superconducting devices.

In this academic paper, we investigate the superconductivity properties of niobium (Nb) thin films in BiOCl/Nb heterostructures. We fabricate Nb thin films on BiOCl substrates with different thicknesses using the dc magnetron sputtering technique. We measure the resistance and temperature dependence of the Nb films and observe superconducting behavior with critical temperatures (Tc) ranging from 7.5 to 9.5 K. We also analyze the effects of film thickness on the superconductivity properties, finding that thicker films have higher Tc values. Finally, we discuss the potential applications of these BiOCl/Nb heterostructures in superconducting devices and further exploration of their properties.

The paper presents a mathematical framework for analyzing the dynamics of parameter-dependent oscillatory networks using the Ott-Antonsen method. The Ott-Antonsen method is a powerful tool for reducing the dimensionality of large-scale networks, allowing for efficient analysis and prediction of their behavior. The authors demonstrate the effectiveness of the method by applying it to various types of parameter-dependent oscillatory networks, including Kuramoto and Stuart-Landau models. The results show that the Ott-Antonsen method accurately captures the dynamics of these networks and provides valuable insights into their behavior. The paper concludes by discussing the implications of these findings for understanding and controlling parameter-dependent oscillatory networks in various fields, such as neuroscience and power systems.

Synchronization is a common phenomenon observed in many complex systems, such as the brain and power grids. Understanding the mechanisms of synchronization and identifying the conditions under which it occurs are important for predicting and controlling the behavior of these systems. In this paper, we study the Ott-Antonsen (OA) method, which is a powerful mathematical framework used to model and analyze synchronization in parameter-independent networks of phase oscillators. However, many real-world networks exhibit parameters that vary spatially or temporally, which can significantly affect the synchronization behavior. In this paper, we extend the OA method to parameter-dependent networks and derive a set of equations that describe the dynamics of not only the phases but also the parameters of the oscillators. We then show how these equations can be used to analyze the stability and attractivity of synchronous states in parameter-dependent networks. Our results demonstrate the utility of the extended OA method for studying synchronization in real-world networks and offer new insights into the interplay between parameter variation and synchronization dynamics.

This paper investigates the concept of almost hypohamiltonian graphs, which are graphs that are almost Hamiltonian but have one missing edge. The authors provide a characterization of almost hypohamiltonian graphs and present several results related to their properties. They also discuss the relationship between almost hypohamiltonian graphs and other types of graphs, such as hypohamiltonian graphs and almost Hamiltonian graphs. The paper concludes with open questions and directions for future research in this area.

This paper discusses the concept of almost hypohamiltonian graphs, which are graphs that are close to satisfying the hypohamiltonian property of having a Hamiltonian cycle with one missing edge. The paper explores the properties and characteristics of these graphs, including their minimum degree, connectivity, and potential structural properties. The authors also provide examples and counterexamples of almost hypohamiltonian graphs and prove some results related to their existence. Additionally, the paper examines the computational complexity of determining whether a graph is almost hypohamiltonian and discusses potential areas for further research in this area.

This paper presents improvements in the asymptotic analysis of coefficients of multivariate generating functions at smooth points. The authors propose a new method that combines singularity analysis and complex analysis techniques to obtain more accurate asymptotic estimates. The method is applied to several examples, including combinatorial problems and random walks, and the results show significant improvements compared to previous approaches. The paper also discusses the limitations of the method and suggests directions for future research. Overall, this work contributes to the field of asymptotic analysis and provides a valuable tool for analyzing multivariate generating functions at smooth points.

Generating functions are a powerful tool in combinatorics for studying sequences of numbers. In this paper, we study the asymptotic behavior of the coefficients of multivariate generating functions, focusing on the case of smooth points. We present improved bounds for these coefficients, allowing for a more precise analysis of their growth. Our results provide a deeper understanding of the structure of multivariate generating functions, and contribute to the broader field of combinatorial analysis.

The paper presents a coherent-state approach for the Majorana representation, which is a mathematical tool used to describe quantum states of a system. The authors propose a method to construct coherent states in the Majorana representation and derive their properties. They also discuss the application of these coherent states in the study of quantum systems, such as the calculation of expectation values and the description of quantum dynamics. The results obtained using the coherent-state approach are compared with those obtained using other methods, demonstrating the effectiveness and usefulness of this approach. Overall, the paper provides a comprehensive and detailed analysis of the coherent-state approach for the Majorana representation and its applications in quantum physics.

In this paper, we present a coherent-state approach for the Majorana representation. We introduce a general formalism for the construction of coherent states associated with a given Lie algebra, and apply it to the construction of coherent states for the Majorana representation. We show that these coherent states capture the geometric and algebraic properties of the Majorana representation, and we derive the corresponding semiclassical limit. We also demonstrate the usefulness of our coherent-state approach by applying it to the case of the isotropic oscillator, and we discuss possible extensions and applications of our formalism. Our results provide a new and powerful tool for the study of the Majorana representation and its applications in quantum mechanics.

In this paper, we investigate the dynamics of two driven, dissipative, nonlinear cavities coupled with single and two-photon exchange. We analyze the spin squeezing, entanglement, and coherence properties of the system under different driving and dissipation conditions. We find that the system exhibits rich dynamics, including the generation of spin squeezing and entanglement, as well as the preservation of coherence. We also explore the effects of different parameters on these properties and discuss potential applications of our findings in quantum information processing and quantum metrology.

In this paper, we investigate the spin squeezing, entanglement, and coherence properties of a system consisting of two driven, dissipative, nonlinear cavities coupled with single and two-photon exchange. We derive analytical expressions for the spin squeezing parameter and the entanglement concurrence in terms of the system parameters such as the driving strengths, dissipation rates, and nonlinearity strengths. We also calculate the time evolution of the system's coherence and show that it exhibits periodic behavior. Our results indicate that the system can exhibit robust spin squeezing and entanglement even in the presence of dissipation and nonlinearity. This work provides valuable insights into the quantum properties of driven, dissipative, nonlinear cavity systems and could have implications for quantum information processing and quantum communication applications.

This paper provides a comprehensive review of reconfigurable reflectarrays and array lenses for dynamic antenna beam control. Reflectarrays and array lenses are two types of antenna systems that can be used to dynamically control the direction of the antenna beam. Reflectarrays consist of an array of individually controllable elements that can be adjusted to steer the beam in different directions. Array lenses, on the other hand, use a combination of phase shifting and focusing elements to achieve beam control. The paper discusses the design principles, fabrication techniques, and performance characteristics of both reflectarrays and array lenses. It also provides an overview of the various applications of these antenna systems, including satellite communications, radar systems, and wireless networks. The paper concludes with a discussion of the challenges and future directions in the field of reconfigurable reflectarrays and array lenses for dynamic antenna beam control.

This paper provides a comprehensive review of reconfigurable reflectarrays and array lenses for dynamic antenna beam control. These technologies play a crucial role in modern wireless communication systems, allowing for rapid adaptation of the antenna beam to changing environmental conditions and communication requirements. The paper discusses the principles, design considerations, and performance metrics of reconfigurable reflectarrays and array lenses. It also presents an overview of the recent advancements in materials, fabrication techniques, and integration methods for these devices. Finally, the paper highlights the challenges and potential future directions in the field, aiming to inspire further research and development in dynamic antenna beam control.

This paper presents a proof of the O-minimal Hauptvermutung for polyhedra, which states that every definable set in an o-minimal structure is a finite union of polyhedra. The proof builds on previous work by introducing a new technique called "cellular decomposition with respect to a definable function". This technique allows for the decomposition of definable sets into cells, which are then further decomposed into polyhedra. The paper also discusses the implications of this result for the study of o-minimal structures and provides examples and applications of the O-minimal Hauptvermutung for polyhedra.

This paper examines the O-minimal Hauptvermutung for polyhedra, building upon the previous work in the field. The O-minimal Hauptvermutung is a conjecture in geometry that states that every definable set in an o-minimal structure is a finite union of polyhedra. In this paper, we focus specifically on the case of polyhedra and provide a proof of the conjecture for this class of objects. Our proof relies on a combination of techniques from model theory and algebraic topology, and provides a deeper understanding of the relationship between o-minimal structures and polyhedra. The results obtained in this paper have implications for the fields of model theory, algebraic geometry, and topology, and contribute to our understanding of the underlying structure of definable sets in o-minimal structures.

The abstract for the academic paper titled "$^{24}$Mg($p$, $\alpha$)$^{21}$Na reaction study for spectroscopy of $^{21}$Na" is as follows: This paper presents a study on the $^{24}$Mg($p$, $\alpha$)$^{21}$Na reaction for the purpose of spectroscopy of $^{21}$Na. The $^{21}$Na nucleus is of interest due to its potential applications in nuclear astrophysics and nuclear structure studies. The experimental setup involved the use of a proton beam on a $^{24}$Mg target, and the resulting $\alpha$ particles were detected and analyzed. The excitation energy spectrum of $^{21}$Na was obtained, and several excited states were identified. The results provide valuable information for understanding the nuclear structure of $^{21}$Na and its role in astrophysical processes.

The paper focuses on the study of the $^{24}$Mg($p$, $\alpha$)$^{21}$Na reaction in order to gain a better understanding of the spectroscopy of the nucleus $^{21}$Na. The experimental setup and methodology used in the study are described, including the use of a magnetic spectrograph and the measurement of differential cross sections. The results of the experiment are presented and analyzed, including the identification of excited states in $^{21}$Na and the determination of their energies, spin, and parity. The implications of these findings for nuclear structure and reactions are discussed. Overall, the study provides valuable insights into the properties of $^{21}$Na and contributes to the broader field of nuclear physics.

Aperture synthesis imaging is a powerful technique used in radio astronomy to achieve high-resolution images of celestial objects. However, the field of view (FOV) of traditional aperture synthesis imaging is limited by the size of the individual antennas and the spacing between them. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to increase the FOV of near-field aperture synthesis imaging by utilizing a larger number of antennas and optimizing their placement. We present a theoretical analysis of the FOV for near-field aperture synthesis imaging and derive an expression for the maximum achievable FOV. We also discuss the practical considerations and challenges in implementing this approach. Our results show that by increasing the number of antennas and optimizing their placement, it is possible to significantly increase the FOV of near-field aperture synthesis imaging, enabling the study of larger regions of the sky with high resolution. This has important implications for future radio astronomy observations and can lead to new discoveries in the field.

The paper discusses the importance and calculation of the field of view (FOV) in near-field aperture synthesis imaging. Near-field imaging is crucial for a wide range of applications, including biomedical imaging and non-destructive testing. The FOV defines the maximum spatial extent of the object that can be imaged within a certain imaging system. The paper presents a comprehensive analysis of the FOV and its dependence on various parameters such as the wavelength, aperture size, and distance from the object. The authors also propose a novel method to estimate the FOV considering different imaging system configurations. Theoretical calculations and experimental validations are presented to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results obtained in this study provide valuable insights into optimizing near-field aperture synthesis imaging systems and improving image reconstruction quality.

Diffusion spectrum imaging (DSI) and multishell diffusion imaging (MSDI) are powerful techniques for studying brain connectivity and microstructure. However, the analysis of DSI and MSDI data can be challenging due to the complex nature of the data and the lack of user-friendly tools. In this paper, we propose a method called Generalized Q-Sampling Conversion (GQSC) to increase the analytical accessibility of DSI and MSDI data. GQSC converts DSI and MSDI data into a more easily interpretable format, allowing for the application of existing analysis techniques. We demonstrate the effectiveness of GQSC on both simulated and real DSI and MSDI datasets, showing improved visualization and analysis capabilities. Our method has the potential to enhance the utilization of DSI and MSDI data in neuroscience research and clinical applications.

Diffusion imaging techniques, such as multishell and diffusion spectrum imaging (DSI), have become valuable tools for studying brain connectivity. However, the analysis of these data can be complex and computationally demanding.
